questions.question: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation states that the gravitational force between two masses is:
options: Inversely proportional to the square of the product of their masses
Inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them
Inversely proportional to the product of their masses
Inversely proportional to the distance between them
questions.explanation: $F = GMm/r^2$. The force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance — this is the inverse square law.

questions.question: Two $50\text{ kg}$ masses are $2.0\text{ m}$ apart. What is the gravitational force between them? ($G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\text{ N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$)
If the distance between two masses is doubled, the gravitational force between them:
questions.explanation: $F \propto 1/r^2$. Doubling $r$ means $F$ becomes $1/2^2 = 1/4$ of the original force.

questions.question: The gravitational force between two equal masses $2.0\text{ m}$ apart is $1.67 \times 10^{-9}\text{ N}$. What is each mass? ($G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\text{ N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$)
The gravitational force between a $100\text{ kg}$ mass and a $200\text{ kg}$ mass is $5.34 \times 10^{-8}\text{ N}$. How far apart are they? ($G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\text{ N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$)
A planet has twice the mass of Earth and the same radius. What is its surface gravity? ($g_{Earth} = 9.8\text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.explanation: $g \propto M/r^2$. Same radius, double mass → double $g$. $g = 2 \times 9.8 = 19.6\text{ m/s}^2$.

questions.question: An astronaut weighs $800\text{ N}$ on Earth ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$). On Planet Y, where $g = 5.0\text{ m/s}^2$, what is her weight?
questions.explanation: Mass $= 800/10 = 80\text{ kg}$ (constant). Weight on Planet Y $= 80 \times 5.0 = 400\text{ N}$.

questions.question: A satellite weighs $10,000\text{ N}$ at Earth's surface. At an altitude equal to Earth's radius ($2R_E$ from Earth's center), what is its weight?
questions.explanation: $F \propto 1/r^2$. Doubling the distance from Earth's center reduces the force to $1/4$. $10,000/4 = 2,500\text{ N}$.

questions.question: Planet P has mass $M$ and Planet Q has mass $3M$, at the same distance from a star. How does the gravitational force on Q compare to P?
questions.explanation: $F \propto m$. Triple the planet mass → triple the gravitational force from the star.

questions.question: A person weighs $600\text{ N}$ at Earth's surface. What is their weight at an altitude of one Earth radius above the surface?
questions.explanation: At altitude $R_E$, distance from center $= 2R_E$. $F \propto 1/r^2$. Force $= 600/4 = 150\text{ N}$.

questions.question: Planet A has the same mass as Planet B but twice the radius. How does $g_A$ compare to $g_B$?
questions.explanation: $g \propto M/r^2$. Same mass, double radius → $g_A = g_B/2^2 = g_B/4$.

questions.question: The universal gravitational constant $G$ has the value:
questions.explanation: $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\text{ N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$. The other options are $g$, Avogadro's number, and the speed of light.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered Newton's Law of Gravitation and the inverse square law!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review how gravitational force changes with distance and mass.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on $F = GMm/r^2$, $g = GM/r^2$, and proportional reasoning.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: the inverse square law, $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}$, and how weight changes with altitude.
